llcFilesWith: Indiana Secretary of State through INBiz (Certificate of Assumed Business Name)
soleProprietorFilesWith: County recorder in each county where the business is conducted (individuals and general partnerships do not file with the Secretary of State)
feeBasis: The $20 Certificate of Assumed Business Name is filed with the Secretary of State by an LLC or corporation; INBiz charges $21 online and mailed filings are $30. Sole proprietors and general partnerships instead pay the recording fee set by their county recorder.
renewalRule: An assumed business name does not expire and is not renewed; it stays in effect until the entity files a notice of discontinuance (no fee by mail, $2 online).
protectionNote: Registering an assumed business name discloses the name an entity trades under; it grants no exclusive trademark right. It carries a practical effect at the state level: a registered assumed business name is part of the § 23-0.5-3-1 distinguishability records, so it blocks a later entity or assumed name that is not distinguishable from it while active. County-recorded assumed names filed by sole proprietors carry no statewide protection and are not centrally indexed.
searchNote: Assumed business names adopted by LLCs and corporations appear in the Secretary of State's INBiz business search alongside the entities, because they share the state entity records. County-recorded assumed names for sole proprietors and general partnerships sit in each county recorder's office and are not in the state search.
notes: Indiana splits its DBA system. LLCs and corporations file a Certificate of Assumed Business Name centrally with the Secretary of State through INBiz ($20; $21 online, $30 by mail), where it joins the state entity records and the distinguishability screen and never expires. Sole proprietors and general partnerships instead record an assumed-name certificate with the county recorder in each county where they do business. There is no newspaper-publication requirement at either level.
